caveats: a: BLS occupation counts cover all NAICS 325 chemical manufacturing rather than this six-digit code or the eligible contract-production subset.
a: ILO exposure is global and focused on generative AI; it is not an observed displacement measure.
a: The estimate excludes already automated distributed-control and conventional process-control tasks.
rho: No source measures five-year implementation for small inorganic contract manufacturers.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply with electronic batch records, historian quality, and customer validation requirements.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the service-business lens.
e: Public descriptions often do not reveal whether production is toll, captive, or proprietary.
s5: The Census figure covers responding owners of employer businesses across industries and is based on 2018 data.
s5: Age, intent to exit, completed sale, and transferable operating condition are different quantities.
q: No public contract sample establishes pass-through behavior for eligible firms.
q: Commodity-linked pricing and customer concentration can make capture much lower than gross operational benefit.
d5: EIA's bulk-chemical outlook is broader than inorganic chemicals and reports energy and feedstock consumption rather than service quantity.
d5: Constant-quality demand is not directly observed, and the five-year result can be dominated by the industrial cycle.
o: The estimate assumes the current service basket and does not protect individual chemicals from regulatory phaseout or technological substitution.
o: Accountable operation may consolidate into larger plants even when physical demand persists.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial customers pay per batch, unit, or contracted production campaign, often with specification, testing, documentation, and hazardous-material handling embedded in the price.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines large continuous commodity plants with contract and specialty-batch operations. The lens retains only recurring outsourced contract or toll production so that firm eligibility and value capture describe one coherent business model.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated or captive plants, businesses selling only proprietary commodity output, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market toll, contract, and custom producers that repeatedly synthesize, purify, blend, or regenerate basic inorganic chemicals for external customers under recurring specifications.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader chemical-manufacturing occupation mix, not NAICS 325180 contract producers, while ILO measures GenAI task exposure by broad occupation globally. I map higher exposure to administrative, commercial, laboratory-documentation, and engineering work and low exposure to plant operation, packaging, maintenance, and material movement, then bound the wage-weighted result. The broader chemical mix may overstate exposure if small inorganic plants employ a larger production share, while GenAI-only evidence may understate machine-vision and process-control opportunity.
caveats: BLS occupation counts cover all NAICS 325 chemical manufacturing rather than this six-digit code or the eligible contract-production subset.
ILO exposure is global and focused on generative AI; it is not an observed displacement measure.
The estimate excludes already automated distributed-control and conventional process-control tasks.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from at least ten eligible 325180 toll or custom plants, matched to O*NET tasks and current automation status, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Chemical manufacturing employs many physical production roles: BLS reports 124,330 chemical equipment operators and 57,400 packaging and filling operators in 2025, alongside smaller technical and scientific groups. AI can materially assist scheduling, purchasing, documentation, quality review, quoting, and laboratory knowledge work, but cannot directly replace most hazardous physical handling and control-room accountability.

d5.bridge: EIA projects growing industrial liquid-fuel and bulk-chemical feedstock use across its long-run cases, not constant-price output of NAICS 325180 custom inorganic services over exactly five years. I translate that broad signal into near-flat modest real quantity growth and widen the downside for product-specific substitution, imports, and industrial cyclicality. Feedstock growth may overstate this code's demand because much of it is petrochemical rather than inorganic.
caveats: EIA's bulk-chemical outlook is broader than inorganic chemicals and reports energy and feedstock consumption rather than service quantity.
Constant-quality demand is not directly observed, and the five-year result can be dominated by the industrial cycle.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level shipment volumes, customer end-market forecasts, import penetration, capacity announcements, and backlog or volume data from a representative sample of eligible plants would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Inorganic chemicals remain physical inputs to water treatment, electronics, construction, industrial processing, and other downstream activity, but the code is cyclical and vulnerable to imports, plant closures, environmental restrictions, and customer reformulation. The EIA signal supports durability more than rapid growth.

caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the service-business lens.
Public descriptions often do not reveal whether production is toll, captive, or proprietary.
e.change_evidence: A plant-by-plant classification of the 119 dataset firms using customer contracts, product catalogs, ownership, revenue concentration, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Most basic inorganic output is produced as commodity chemicals in dedicated continuous plants, while the eligible population is limited to independent custom, toll, regeneration, or specification-driven producers serving external customers. EPA's sector profile distinguishes large continuous commodity plants from smaller as-needed specialty-batch operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325180 ($5,072,657,421) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325180 ($42,528,553,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $1,017,722 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325180); class <5: 58 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,435,914 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $258,608 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,656,171 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $658,476 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,854,951 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,234,577 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 78 firms, avg revenue/firm $41,049,231 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,392,966 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $141,278,185 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,444,201 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $348,581,082 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,779,453 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 119

caveats: The estimate assumes the current service basket and does not protect individual chemicals from regulatory phaseout or technological substitution.
Accountable operation may consolidate into larger plants even when physical demand persists.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and procurement records identifying insourcing plans, direct self-service alternatives, formulation substitution, and fully autonomous production roadmaps would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Customers will still require physical chemical conversion, testing, safe handling, environmental controls, and accountable release of product. Software may reduce coordination and documentation labor but does not supply the chemical or bear plant and compliance responsibility; the principal displacement risk is customer insourcing or product substitution.

caveats: No public contract sample establishes pass-through behavior for eligible firms.
Commodity-linked pricing and customer concentration can make capture much lower than gross operational benefit.
q.change_evidence: A sample of customer contracts showing term, renewal cadence, open-book clauses, cost indices, productivity sharing, minimum volumes, and historical repricing after process improvements would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Specification-bound production and switching costs can preserve part of implemented savings during a contract term, while repeat bids, index adjustments, open-book tolling, and sophisticated industrial procurement transmit savings to customers. Benefits tied to compliance quality and faster turnaround are more defensible than direct labor removal.

caveats: No source measures five-year implementation for small inorganic contract manufacturers.
Implementation will vary sharply with electronic batch records, historian quality, and customer validation requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level pilots measuring accepted AI recommendations, exception rates, validation time, and labor hours before and after deployment across quality, scheduling, maintenance, and customer-service workflows would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Document-heavy office and quality workflows can be implemented within five years, but hazardous-process validation, cybersecurity, data fragmentation, auditability, and operator training slow deployment. The EPA's 2026 area-source amendments reinforce that compliance records and accountable controls remain operational constraints.

s5.bridge: The Census Annual Business Survey reports owner age for responding U.S. employer businesses across industries, not eligible 325180 firms or completed control transfers. I use the 51% share of owners age 55 and older only as a succession-pressure signal, then reduce it substantially for a five-year window, multi-owner firms, internal succession, non-sale outcomes, and operations that cannot transfer. The all-industry proxy may overstate sale probability for capital-intensive regulated plants, although aging technical owners could increase succession pressure.
caveats: The Census figure covers responding owners of employer businesses across industries and is based on 2018 data.
Age, intent to exit, completed sale, and transferable operating condition are different quantities.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year closed-transaction census from chemical-sector deal databases, state permit ownership changes, and interviews with lower-middle-market chemical M&A advisers, restricted to eligible toll and custom plants, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership creates potential supply, but owner age is not a transaction. Environmental liabilities, specialized permits, customer approvals, and plant capital intensity can prevent or delay qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring specification-bound production creates defensible demand and valuable administrative, quality, and planning workflows for AI assistance.
principal_weakness: The eligible contract-production subset may be very small and carries capital, compliance, and environmental liabilities that AI does not remove.
